[newspapers](https://www.google.com/search?&q=%22Liquefied+natural+gas%22&tbs=bkt:s&tbm=bks) **·** [books](https://www.google.com/search?tbs=bks:1&q=%22Liquefied+natural+gas%22+-wikipedia) **·** [scholar](https://scholar.google.com/scholar?q=%22Liquefied+natural+gas%22) **·** [JSTOR](https://www.jstor.org/action/doBasicSearch?Query=%22Liquefied+natural+gas%22&acc=on&wc=on) *(February 2026)* *([Learn how and when to remove this message](/wiki/Help:Maintenance_template_removal "Help:Maintenance template removal"))* |

(**LNG**) is [natural gas](/wiki/Natural_gas "Natural gas") (predominantly [methane](/wiki/Methane "Methane"), CH4, with some mixture of [ethane](/wiki/Ethane "Ethane"), C2H6) that has been cooled to liquid form for ease and safety of non-pressurized storage or transport. It takes up about 1/600th the volume of natural gas in the gaseous state at [standard temperature and pressure](/wiki/Standard_temperature_and_pressure "Standard temperature and pressure").

emerged in the 1960s. Engineers at [Phillips Petroleum Company](/wiki/Phillips_Petroleum_Company "Phillips Petroleum Company") developed the *Optimized Cascade Process*, which employed three successive refrigeration cycles—propane for precooling, ethylene (or ethane) for intermediate cooling, and methane for final liquefaction. This stepwise approach improved thermodynamic efficiency and operational stability. It was first applied commercially at the Kenai LNG plant in Alaska in 1969, the first baseload LNG export facility in the United States.

From Wikipedia, the free encyclopedia

Form of natural gas for easier storage and transport

"LNG" redirects here. For other uses, see [LNG (disambiguation)](/wiki/LNG_(disambiguation) "LNG (disambiguation)").

Not to be confused with [liquefied petroleum gas](/wiki/Liquefied_petroleum_gas "Liquefied petroleum gas"), nor with [compressed natural gas](/wiki/Compressed_natural_gas "Compressed natural gas"), nor with [natural-gas condensate](/wiki/Natural-gas_condensate "Natural-gas condensate") (natural gas liquids).

**Liquefied natural gas** (**LNG**) is [natural gas](/wiki/Natural_gas "Natural gas") (predominantly [methane](/wiki/Methane "Methane"), CH4, with some mixture of [ethane](/wiki/Ethane "Ethane"), C2H6) that has been cooled to liquid form for ease and safety of non-pressurized storage or transport. It takes up about 1/600th the volume of natural gas in the gaseous state at [standard temperature and pressure](/wiki/Standard_temperature_and_pressure "Standard temperature and pressure").

LNG is [odorless](/wiki/Odorless "Odorless"), [colorless](/wiki/Transparency_and_translucency "Transparency and translucency"), [non-toxic](/wiki/Toxicity "Toxicity"), and [non-corrosive](/wiki/Corrosive_substance "Corrosive substance"). Hazards include flammability after vaporization into a gaseous state, freezing and [asphyxia](/wiki/Asphyxia "Asphyxia"). The [liquefaction](/wiki/Liquefaction_of_gases "Liquefaction of gases") process involves removal of certain components, such as dust, [acid gases](/wiki/Acid_gas "Acid gas"), [helium](/wiki/Helium "Helium"), water, and heavy [hydrocarbons](/wiki/Hydrocarbon "Hydrocarbon"), which could cause difficulty downstream. The natural gas is then [condensed](/wiki/Condensation "Condensation") into a liquid at close to atmospheric pressure by cooling it to approximately −162 °C (−260 °F); maximum transport pressure is set at around 127 kPa (18 psi) ([gauge pressure](/wiki/Gauge_pressure "Gauge pressure")), which is about 1.25 times atmospheric pressure at sea level. The gas stream is typically separated into the liquefied petroleum fractions ([butane](/wiki/Butane "Butane") and [propane](/wiki/Propane "Propane")) which can be stored in liquid form at relatively low pressure, and the lighter [ethane](/wiki/Ethane "Ethane") and [methane](/wiki/Methane "Methane") fractions. These lighter fractions of methane and ethane are then liquefied to make up the bulk of LNG that is shipped.

Before the late 20th century natural gas was largely considered a byproduct of oil production. The development of production processes, [cryogenic storage](/wiki/Cryogenic_storage "Cryogenic storage"), and transportation made it possible to commercialize natural gas, creating a global market which now competes with other fuels. The development of LNG storage made natural gas transportation much more reliable. Unlike simple tank storage used for other fuels, natural gas previously could not be stored for extended periods due to the difficulty of preventing gas leakage. Large-scale cryogenic storage made it possible to create reliable long-term storage reserves for natural gas as well. These reserves of liquefied gas could be quickly deployed through [regasification](/wiki/Regasification "Regasification") processes, and today are the main means for networks to handle local [peak shaving](/wiki/Peak_shaving "Peak shaving") requirements.[[1]](#cite_note-1)

Production of LNG is an energy intensive process concentrated in a few countries, and typically requires specialized ports for handling the export of the LNG for use in other countries. As of 2023, the United States, Australia and Qatar had the most capacity for exporting LNG, and China, Japan, and South Korea were the biggest importers.[[2]](#cite_note-2) A 2025 report by the IEA found that more capacity would be coming on in the upcoming decade.[[3]](#cite_note-3) This hyper-concentration of production creates choke points in global supply chains, with the [2026 War in Iran affecting Qatar's LNG exports](/wiki/Economic_impact_of_the_2026_Iran_war "Economic impact of the 2026 Iran war"), causing a ripple effect in LNG access and cost.[[4]](#cite_note-4)

## Specific energy content and energy density

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Liquefied_natural_gas&action=edit&section=1 "Edit section: Specific energy content and energy density")]

The [heating value](/wiki/Heating_value "Heating value") depends on the source of gas that is used and the process that is used to liquefy the gas. The range of heating value can span ±10 to 15 percent. A typical value of the [higher heating value](/wiki/Higher_heating_value "Higher heating value") of LNG is approximately 50 MJ/kg or 21,500 BTU/lb.[[5]](#cite_note-5) A typical value of the [lower heating value](/wiki/Lower_heating_value "Lower heating value") of LNG is 45 MJ/kg or 19,350 BTU/lb.

For the purpose of comparison of different fuels, the heating value may be expressed in terms of energy per volume, which is known as the [energy density](/wiki/Energy_density "Energy density") expressed in MJ/litre. The density of LNG is roughly 0.41 kg/litre to 0.5 kg/litre, depending on temperature, pressure, and composition,[[6]](#cite_note-6) compared to water at 1.0 kg/litre. Using the median value of 0.45 kg/litre, the typical energy density values are 22.5 MJ/litre (based on higher heating value) or 20.3 MJ/litre (based on lower heating value).

The volumetric energy density of LNG is approximately 2.4 times that of [compressed natural gas](/wiki/Compressed_natural_gas "Compressed natural gas") (CNG), which makes it economical to transport natural gas by ship in the form of LNG. The energy density of LNG is comparable to [propane](/wiki/Propane "Propane") and [ethanol](/wiki/Ethanol "Ethanol") but is only 60 percent that of [diesel](/wiki/Diesel_fuel "Diesel fuel") and 70 percent that of [gasoline](/wiki/Gasoline "Gasoline").[[7]](#cite_note-7)

## History

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Liquefied_natural_gas&action=edit&section=2 "Edit section: History")]

By the middle of the seventeenth century [Robert Boyle](/wiki/Robert_Boyle "Robert Boyle") had derived the inverse relationship between the pressure and the volume of gases. About the same time, [Guillaume Amontons](/wiki/Guillaume_Amontons "Guillaume Amontons") was investigating the effect of temperature on gases. Early in the nineteenth century [Cagniard de la Tour](/wiki/Cagniard_de_la_Tour "Cagniard de la Tour") showed there was a temperature above which a gas could not be liquefied. In 1886 [Karol Olszewski](/wiki/Karol_Olszewski "Karol Olszewski") liquefied methane, the primary constituent of natural gas.[[8]](#cite_note-8)

The first large-scale liquefaction of natural gas in the U.S. was in 1918 when the U.S. government liquefied natural gas as a way to extract helium, which is a small component of some natural gas. This helium was intended for use in British dirigibles for [World War I](/wiki/World_War_I "World War I"). The liquid natural gas (LNG) was not stored, but regasified and immediately put into the gas mains.[[9]](#cite_note-9)

Because of large volumes it is not practical to store natural gas, as a gas, near atmospheric pressure. However, when liquefied, it can be stored in a volume 1/600th as large. This is a practical way to store it but the gas must be kept at −260 °F (−162 °C).[*[citation needed](/wiki/Wikipedia:Citation_needed "Wikipedia:Citation needed")*]

The key patents having to do with natural gas liquefaction date from 1915 and the mid-1930s. In 1915, [Godfrey Cabot](/wiki/Godfrey_Lowell_Cabot "Godfrey Lowell Cabot") patented a method for storing liquid gases at very low temperatures. It consisted of a [Thermos bottle](/wiki/Thermos_bottle "Thermos bottle")-type design which included a cold inner tank within an outer tank, the tanks being separated by insulation.[[10]](#cite_note-10)[[11]](#cite_note-11)

In 1937 Lee Twomey received patents for a process for large-scale liquefaction of natural gas. The intention was to store natural gas as a liquid so it could be used to support peak energy loads during cold snaps.
Twomey used a variant of the [Linde process](/wiki/Hampson%E2%80%93Linde_cycle "Hampson–Linde cycle"). In this process, the gas is cooled regeneratively by continually passing and expanding it through an orifice until it is cooled to temperatures at which it liquefies. This process was developed by James Joule and William Thomson and is known as the [Joule–Thomson effect](/wiki/Joule%E2%80%93Thomson_effect "Joule–Thomson effect").[[12]](#cite_note-12)[[13]](#cite_note-13)[[14]](#cite_note-14) A variation of the Linde process, called the Claude process, is sometimes used.

The **cascade process** emerged in the 1960s. Engineers at [Phillips Petroleum Company](/wiki/Phillips_Petroleum_Company "Phillips Petroleum Company") developed the *Optimized Cascade Process*, which employed three successive refrigeration cycles—propane for precooling, ethylene (or ethane) for intermediate cooling, and methane for final liquefaction. This stepwise approach improved thermodynamic efficiency and operational stability. It was first applied commercially at the Kenai LNG plant in Alaska in 1969, the first baseload LNG export facility in the United States.

The [East Ohio Gas](/wiki/East_Ohio_Gas "East Ohio Gas") Company built a full-scale commercial LNG plant in [Cleveland, Ohio](/wiki/Cleveland,_Ohio "Cleveland, Ohio"), in 1940 just after a successful pilot plant built by its sister company, Hope Natural Gas Company of West Virginia. This was the first such plant in the world. Originally it had three spheres, approximately 63 feet (19 m) in diameter containing LNG at −260 °F (−162 °C). Each sphere held the equivalent of about 50 million cubic foot (1,400,000 m3) of natural gas. A fourth tank, a cylinder, was added in 1942. It had an equivalent capacity of 100 million cubic foot (2,800,000 m3) of gas. The plant operated successfully for three years. The stored gas was regasified and put into the mains when cold snaps hit and extra capacity was needed. This precluded the denial of gas to some customers during a cold snap.

The [Cleveland plant failed](/wiki/Cleveland_East_Ohio_Gas_explosion "Cleveland East Ohio Gas explosion") on October 20, 1944, when the cylindrical tank ruptured, spilling thousands of gallons of LNG over the plant and nearby neighborhood. The gas evaporated and caught fire, which caused 130 fatalities.[[17]](#cite_note-17) The fire delayed further implementation of LNG facilities for several years. However, over the next 15 years new research on low-temperature alloys, and better insulation materials, set the stage for a revival of the industry. It restarted in 1959 when a U.S. World War II [Liberty ship](/wiki/Liberty_ship "Liberty ship"), the *Methane Pioneer*, converted to carry LNG, made a delivery of LNG from the U.S. Gulf Coast to energy-starved Great Britain. In June 1964, the world's first purpose-built LNG carrier, the *Methane Princess*, entered service.[[18]](#cite_note-18) Soon after that[*[when?](/wiki/Wikipedia:Manual_of_Style/Dates_and_numbers#Chronological_items "Wikipedia:Manual of Style/Dates and numbers")*] a large natural gas field was discovered in Algeria. International trade in LNG quickly followed as LNG was shipped to France and Great Britain from the Algerian fields.[*[citation needed](/wiki/Wikipedia:Citation_needed "Wikipedia:Citation needed")*]

The LNG industry in the U.S. restarted in 1965 with the building of a number of new plants, which continued through the 1970s. These plants were not only used for peak-shaving, as in Cleveland, but also for base-load supplies for places that never had natural gas before this. A number of import facilities were built on the East Coast in anticipation of the need to import energy via LNG. However, As of 2010[[update]](https://en.wikipedia.org/w/index.php?title=Liquefied_natural_gas&action=edit) [hydraulic fracturing](/wiki/Hydraulic_fracturing "Hydraulic fracturing") ("fracking") created a boom in U.S. natural gas production, resulting in import facilities being repurposed as export facilities. The first U.S. LNG export was completed in early 2016.[[19]](#cite_note-bizjournals.com-19)

By 2023, the U.S. had become the biggest exporter in the world, and projects already under construction or permitted would double its export capacities by 2027.[[20]](#cite_note-biggest-20) The largest exporters were [Cheniere Energy](/wiki/Cheniere_Energy "Cheniere Energy") Inc., [Freeport LNG](/wiki/Freeport_LNG "Freeport LNG"), and Venture Global LNG Inc.[[21]](#cite_note-top-21) The U.S. Energy Information Administration reported that the U.S. had exported 4.3 trillion cubic foot (1.2×1011 m3) in 2023.[[22]](#cite_note-22)

## LNG life cycle

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Liquefied_natural_gas&action=edit&section=4 "Edit section: LNG life cycle")]

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/c/cf/LNGprocess.png/500px-LNGprocess.png)](/wiki/File:LNGprocess.png)

LNG life cycle

The process begins with the pre-treatment of a feedstock of natural gas entering the system to remove impurities such as [H2S](/wiki/Hydrogen_sulfide "Hydrogen sulfide"), CO2, H2O, [mercury](/wiki/Mercury_(element) "Mercury (element)") and higher-chained [hydrocarbons](/wiki/Hydrocarbon "Hydrocarbon"). Feedstock gas then enters the liquefaction unit where it is cooled to between −145 °C and −163 °C.[[23]](#cite_note-:0-23) Although the type or number of heating cycles and/or [refrigerants](/wiki/Refrigerants "Refrigerants") used may vary based on the technology, the basic process involves circulating the gas through aluminum tube coils and exposure to a compressed refrigerant.[[23]](#cite_note-:0-23) As the refrigerant is vaporized, the heat transfer causes the gas in the coils to cool.[[23]](#cite_note-:0-23) The LNG is then stored in a specialized double-walled insulated tank at [atmospheric pressure](/wiki/Atmospheric_pressure "Atmospheric pressure") ready to be transported to its final destination.[[23]](#cite_note-:0-23)

Most domestic LNG is transported by land via truck/trailer designed for [cryogenic](/wiki/Cryogenic "Cryogenic") temperatures.[[23]](#cite_note-:0-23) Intercontinental LNG transport travels by special tanker ships. LNG transport tanks comprise an internal steel or aluminum compartment and an external carbon or steel compartment with a vacuum system in between to reduce the amount of heat transfer.[[23]](#cite_note-:0-23) Once on site, the LNG must be stored in vacuum insulated or flat bottom [storage tanks](/wiki/Storage_tanks "Storage tanks").[[23]](#cite_note-:0-23) When ready for distribution, the LNG enters a regasification facility where it is pumped into a vaporizer and heated back into gaseous form.[[23]](#cite_note-:0-23) The gas then enters the gas pipeline distribution system and is delivered to the end-user.[[23]](#cite_note-:0-23)

The natural gas fed into the LNG plant will be treated to remove water, [hydrogen sulfide](/wiki/Hydrogen_sulfide "Hydrogen sulfide"), [carbon dioxide](/wiki/Carbon_dioxide "Carbon dioxide"), [benzene](/wiki/Benzene "Benzene") and other components that will [freeze](/wiki/Freezing "Freezing") under the low temperatures needed for storage or be destructive to the liquefaction facility. LNG typically contains more than 90% [methane](/wiki/Methane "Methane"). It also contains small amounts of [ethane](/wiki/Ethane "Ethane"), [propane](/wiki/Propane "Propane"), [butane](/wiki/Butane "Butane"), some heavier [alkanes](/wiki/Alkane "Alkane"), and nitrogen. The purification process can be designed to give almost 100% [methane](/wiki/Methane "Methane"). One of the risks of LNG is [a rapid phase transition explosion](/wiki/Rapid_phase_transition "Rapid phase transition") (RPT), which occurs when cold LNG comes into contact with [water](/wiki/Water "Water").[[24]](#cite_note-24)

The most important infrastructure needed for LNG production and transportation is an LNG plant consisting of one or more LNG trains, each of which is an independent unit for gas liquefaction and purification. A typical train consists of a compression area, [propane](/wiki/Propane "Propane") condenser area, and [methane](/wiki/Methane "Methane") and [ethane](/wiki/Ethane "Ethane") areas.

The largest LNG train in operation is in Qatar, with a total production capacity of 7.8 million tonnes per annum (MTPA). LNG is loaded onto ships and delivered to a regasification terminal, where the LNG is allowed to expand and reconvert into gas. Regasification terminals are usually connected to a storage and pipeline distribution network to distribute natural gas to local distribution companies (LDCs) or independent power plants (IPPs).

The LNG industry developed slowly during the second half of the last century because most LNG plants are located in remote areas not served by pipelines, and because of the high costs of treating and transporting LNG. Constructing an LNG plant costs at least $1.5 billion per 1 MTPA capacity, a receiving terminal costs $1 billion per 1 bcf/day throughput capacity and LNG vessels cost $200 million–$300 million.

In the early 2000s, prices for constructing LNG plants, receiving terminals and vessels fell as new technologies emerged and more players invested in liquefaction and regasification. This tended to make LNG more competitive as a means of energy distribution, but increasing material costs and demand for construction contractors have put upward pressure on prices in the last few years.
The standard price for a 125,000 cubic meter LNG vessel built in European and Japanese shipyards used to be US$250 million. When Korean and Chinese shipyards entered the race, increased competition reduced profit margins and improved efficiency—reducing costs by 60 percent. Costs in US dollars also declined due to the devaluation of the currencies of the world's largest shipbuilders: the Japanese yen and Korean won.

Since 2004, the large number of orders increased demand for shipyard slots, raising their price and increasing ship costs. The per-ton construction cost of an LNG liquefaction plant fell steadily from the 1970s through the 1990s. The cost reduced by approximately 35 percent. However, recently the cost of building liquefaction and regasification terminals doubled due to increased cost of materials and a shortage of skilled labor, professional engineers, designers, managers and other white-collar professionals.

Due to natural gas shortage concerns in the northeastern U.S. and surplus natural gas in the rest of the country, many new LNG import and export terminals are being contemplated in the United States. Concerns about the safety of such facilities create controversy in some regions where they are proposed. One such location is in the Long Island Sound between Connecticut and Long Island. [Broadwater Energy](/wiki/Broadwater_Energy "Broadwater Energy"), an effort of [TransCanada Corp.](/wiki/TransCanada_Corp. "TransCanada Corp.") and Shell, wishes to build an LNG import terminal in the sound on the New York side. Local politicians including the Suffolk County Executive raised questions about the terminal. In 2005, New York Senators [Chuck Schumer](/wiki/Chuck_Schumer "Chuck Schumer") and [Hillary Clinton](/wiki/Hillary_Clinton "Hillary Clinton") also announced their opposition to the project.[[36]](#cite_note-36) Several import terminal proposals along the coast of [Maine](/wiki/Maine "Maine") were also met with high levels of resistance and questions. On September 13, 2013, the U.S. Department of Energy approved [Dominion Cove Point](/wiki/Dominion_Cove_Point_LNG "Dominion Cove Point LNG")'s application to export up to 770 million cubic foot (22,000,000 m3) per day of LNG to countries that do not have a free trade agreement with the U.S.[[37]](#cite_note-ogj.com-37) In May 2014, the FERC concluded its environmental assessment of the Cove Point LNG project, which found that the proposed natural gas export project could be built and operated safely.[[38]](#cite_note-38) Another LNG terminal is currently proposed for [Elba Island](/wiki/Elba_Island_(Georgia) "Elba Island (Georgia)"), Georgia, US.[[39]](#cite_note-39) Plans for three LNG export terminals in the U.S. Gulf Coast region have also received conditional Federal approval.[[37]](#cite_note-ogj.com-37)[[40]](#cite_note-40) In Canada, an LNG export terminal is under construction near [Guysborough](/wiki/Guysborough "Guysborough"), Nova Scotia.[[41]](#cite_note-41)

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Liquefied_natural_gas&action=edit&section=8 "Edit section: Commercial aspects")]

### Global Trade

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Liquefied_natural_gas&action=edit&section=9 "Edit section: Global Trade")]

|  |  |
| --- | --- |
